_pass: Pass 1 + worked examples v2 + apps + extra exercises + exercises unify v2
title: Stocks: shares, dividends, return
deck: A stock is a tiny ownership stake in a company. Its price moves, and along the way the company may pay dividends. The return on an investment is the sum of both pieces.
objectives: Read a stock quote, compute return on investment as the sum of capital gain and dividend income, and distinguish a stock's price from its underlying business value.

content.text: When a company sells shares of itself to the public, every buyer becomes a partial owner. Apple has approximately 15 billion shares outstanding; owning 100 of them makes you a 15-billionth owner of the company, entitled to your proportional sliver of its profits and a vote at its annual meeting. Most individual investors will never come close to a controlling stake in anything, but the math of ownership scales linearly: 100 shares is 100 times what 1 share is, and the return on a thousand-dollar investment scales accordingly.

Two things can happen between the day you buy a share of stock and the day you sell it. The price can move — up if the market thinks the company is worth more than it did when you bought, down if it thinks less. And the company may pay dividends — regular cash payments to shareholders, funded out of profits. Your total return on the investment is the sum of both effects. This section develops the share-price math, the dividend math, and the combined return-on-investment calculation that lets one compare stock investments to bond investments (Section 7.5) and to any other use of the same capital.

A share is a unit of corporate ownership. Multiplying the number of shares owned by the current price per share gives the dollar value of the position: 100 shares at $50 per share equals $5,000. The share price moves moment-to-moment in the open market, set by the highest price buyers are willing to pay matched against the lowest price sellers are willing to accept.

Dividends are regular cash payments the company makes to each shareholder, usually quarterly, on a per-share basis. If a company pays $0.50 per share each quarter for a year, the annual dividend per share is $2.00, and a holder of 100 shares receives $200 in dividends over the year. Not every company pays dividends; younger, growth-focused companies often reinvest profits internally instead. Mature companies with stable cash flows (utilities, consumer staples, large banks) typically pay substantial dividends.

content.caption: Figure 7.4. A year of stock ownership decomposed. $5,000 buys 100 shares at $50 per share. A year later the price is $58 (capital gain $800) and the company has paid $2 per share in dividends ($200 total). The combined return on the $5,000 investment is $1,000, a 20% return on investment.

content.text: The capital gain on a stock position is the change in share price multiplied by the number of shares: (sale price − purchase price) × shares. The capital gain is unrealized while the shares are still held and realized (and taxable) when sold. The total return on the investment over the holding period is the sum of capital gain and dividends received. Dividing total return by the amount originally invested gives the return on investment, expressed as a percent.

Definition 7.4. The capital gain on a position is (sale price − purchase price) × shares; the dividend income is annual dividend per share × shares. The total return is their sum, and the return on investment (ROI) is total return / amount invested, expressed as a percent.
ROI is the comparable percentage that lets you weigh one investment against another, regardless of the dollar size.

Margin noteRealized capital gains are taxed. The federal capital-gains tax rate is generally lower than the ordinary-income rate (Section 7.2) for long-term holdings (held more than one year); short-term gains (held one year or less) are taxed as ordinary income. Dividends received are typically taxed at the long-term capital-gains rate if the underlying stock has been held long enough; if not, they are taxed as ordinary income. The tax treatment is one of several reasons buy-and-hold investing tends to outperform frequent trading.

Common mistakeReporting return based only on capital gain, ignoring dividends. The price-chart story is highly visible (it is in every financial news ticker), but dividends are real cash flowing into the shareholder's brokerage account every quarter. A stock whose price is flat for the year but pays a 3% dividend has a 3% total return for the year, not a 0% return.

Example 7.4. $5,000 in XYZ stock for one year.

You buy 100 shares of XYZ Corp at $50 per share. One year later, the share price has risen to $58, and during the year XYZ paid $0.50 per share each quarter in dividends. Compute the capital gain, the total dividend income, the total return, and the return on investment.

Solution.

Step 1. Amount invested = 100 × $50 = $5,000.

Step 2. Capital gain = ($58 − $50) × 100 = $800.

Step 3. Annual dividend per share = 4 × $0.50 = $2.00; total dividends = $2.00 × 100 = $200.

Step 4. Total return = $800 + $200 = $1,000.

Step 5. ROI = $1,000 / $5,000 = 0.20 = 20%.

A 20% single-year return is excellent, well above the long-run S&P 500 average of about 10% per year. The decomposition reveals that 80% of the return came from price movement and 20% from dividends, a typical mix for a dividend-paying stock in a good year.

In the wildThe long-run average return on the broad U.S. stock market (the S&P 500 index, a basket of the 500 largest publicly-traded U.S. companies) is approximately 10% per year since World War II, including reinvested dividends. The number masks substantial year-to-year variation: some years up 30%, others down 30% or more. The 10% average is what holds across long periods; any individual year is highly unpredictable. The principle that long-run averages are reliable while individual years are not is the same Law of Large Numbers introduced in Section 6.6.

content.problem: An investor buys 50 shares of ABC at $30 per share. A year later the price is $36 and ABC paid no dividends. Compute the ROI.
An investor buys 200 shares of DEF at $25 per share. A year later the price is $28 and DEF paid $1.50 per share in dividends over the year. Compute the total return in dollars.
An investor buys 40 shares of GHI at $75 per share. A year later the price has fallen to $68, but GHI paid $2.50 per share in dividends. Compute the ROI (to one decimal place).
What does owning a share of a company entitle you to?
content.answer: A partial ownership stake. The shareholder is entitled to a proportional sliver of the company's profits (if and when paid as dividends), to vote at the annual meeting, and to a claim on residual assets if the company is dissolved (after all creditors are paid).

content.problem: True or false: if a stock's price stays the same from purchase to sale but the company pays $1.20 per share in dividends, the investor's total return is zero.
content.answer: False. The total return is positive, equal to the dividend income alone. The price-flat scenario simply means the capital-gain piece is zero; the dividend piece is still real.

content.problem: Why is ROI a more useful comparison metric than total dollar return when comparing two investments of different sizes?
content.answer: ROI is scale-invariant. A $200 gain on a $1,000 investment (20% ROI) outperforms a $1,000 gain on a $20,000 investment (5% ROI), even though the second has a larger dollar gain. The percent return tells you which investment used capital more efficiently.

content.problem: You bought **50 shares** of a stock at **$45/share**. Over the year, the company paid **$1.20/share in dividends**, and you sold at **$52/share**. (a) Dividends received. (b) Capital gain. (c) Total return in dollars and as a percent.
content.answer: (a) Dividends = 50 × 1.20 = **$60**. (b) Capital gain = (52 − 45) × 50 = **$350**. (c) Total return = 350 + 60 = **$410**. Initial investment was 50 × 45 = $2,250. Percent return = 410 / 2,250 × 100 ≈ **18.2%**.

content.problem: A high-dividend utility stock trades at **$72** and pays **$3.60/year** in dividends. What is the dividend yield?

content.title: Dividend yield
content.problem: A company's stock trades at **$60 per share** and pays a **$2.40 annual dividend per share**. What is the **dividend yield**?
content.solution:
**Apply the dividend yield formula.**

yield = (annual dividend / share price) × 100

yield = (2.40 / 60) × 100 = **4%**.

So the company pays an effective **4%** annual cash return on the stock price. A 3–5% yield is typical for mature dividend-paying companies (utilities, banks, consumer staples).

*Note.* The yield changes daily as the share price moves, even though the dividend payment is fixed by the company's board. If the price drops to $50 with the same $2.40 dividend, the yield rises to 4.8%.

content.title: Total return — dividends plus capital gain
content.problem: You buy **100 shares** of a stock at **$50/share** ($5,000 invested). Over the year, the stock pays **$2/share** in dividends, and you sell it at **$58/share**. What is your **total return** in dollars and as a percentage?
content.solution:
**Two components of total return: dividends + capital gain.**

Dividends received: 100 shares × $2 = **$200**.

Capital gain: (58 − 50) × 100 = **$800**.

Total return in dollars = 200 + 800 = **$1,000**.

Percentage return = 1,000 / 5,000 × 100 = **20%**.

*The trap to notice.* If you only counted the capital gain, you'd report a **16% return** ($800/$5,000) and miss the dividends. Total return is always price change *plus* dividends collected. For high-dividend stocks held over long periods, dividends can be 30–50% of the total return.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Stocks have two ways of paying you back: **capital gain** (price appreciation) and **dividends** (regular cash payments from earnings). The **dividend yield** measures the cash payout as a percentage of price; the **total return** combines both into a single performance number. Both are essential for honest comparison of stock investments.

content.title: Stocks in the Robinhood era
content.content: Robinhood, Webull, and similar apps democratized stock investing — fractional shares mean you can buy $5 of Apple. The math of return is the same as it was 100 years ago: total return = capital gain + dividends. But the era introduced new wrinkles: meme stocks (GameStop), 24-hour crypto, payment-for-order-flow. Knowing the math of yield, return, and capital gains is the foundation that makes you a thoughtful participant in this newer, faster-moving market — rather than someone reacting to TikTok hype.

content.text: Stocks are ownership. Section 7.5 introduces the other major asset class, bonds, in which the investor is a lender rather than an owner. The math is different and so is the risk profile; understanding both is the foundation for the diversified-portfolio math of Section 7.6.
